CAISO solar capture — year-on-year trend (January)

SP15 & NP15 hubs · Day-ahead market · January 2023 / 2024 / 2025
The cannibalization thesis: As CAISO solar capacity has grown from ~13 GW (2022) to ~24 GW+ (2025), the capture ratio has declined because more solar competing at the same midday hours depresses the prices solar earns. This is the fundamental long-run valuation risk for solar assets — and the reason battery co-location is increasingly essential to restore value. January is used for consistent YoY comparison: no DST transitions, stable weather baseline, and moderate but meaningful solar penetration.
Hub
Figure 1
Capture ratio trend — January, year on year
Generation-weighted capture price ÷ average LMP. Declining ratio = growing cannibalization discount as solar capacity expands.
Figure 2
Capture price vs avg LMP — by year
The widening gap between average LMP and capture price quantifies the growing value discount.
Figure 3
Duck curve deepening — year on year
Avg hourly SP15 price by year. The midday trough gets deeper each year as solar capacity grows.
Figure 4
Negative-price exposure — growing tail risk
Share of solar generation occurring during negative-price hours. This is the direct curtailment and revenue-loss risk that storage co-location solves.
